Knowing what we've learned so far about the Unity principle, let's return to the account of the concentration camp prisoner I described at the beginning of this module. Recall that the Nazi guard had come to a tenth prisoner in a line with the intention of executing him, but decided to spare his life. Why? It turned out that the guard had recognized the prisoner as being from his hometown. Because of their ties to the same place, the guard felt a sense of unity with the man. Mere mutuality of place turned a murderer into a merciful responder, but only toward one specific unitized individual. This is not the only instance of the unitizing power of place having a dramatic and life-saving effect. Another example also involved Jews during World War II, but it took place thousands of miles away from the prison camp. As the Nazi party gained power in Germany and other parts of Europe during the 1930s and early 1940s, many Jews fled. Some of them even made their way to Japan. The safety of those Jewish refugees became increasingly imperiled though, as Japan became more of a wartime partner with Nazi Germany, which began to press for the mass murder of the Jews residing in Japan. The Nazis went so far as to send the infamous butcher of Warsaw, Josef Mesenger, to Tokyo, so that he could pressure the Japanese government on the issue. Despite this pressure, the Japanese government resisted the Nazi demands all the way through to the end of the war. What could account for the resistance? Part of the answer has to do with a crucial meeting between Japanese officials and leaders of the Jewish community. Facing Nazi demands to execute the Jews but wanting to consider the issue from all sides, high-ranking members of the Japanese military asked the Jewish refugee community to nominate representatives to speak on its behalf at a meeting. Two rabbis were chosen, Moses Shatkes and Shimon Kalash. Each was a respected scholar, but the older man, Rabbi Kalash, was known as a particularly keen observer of human nature. The two rabbis met with a tribunal of Japanese military officers who would determine the fate of their people. The rabbis' diplomatic skills were put to the test immediately when members of the tribunal quickly asked two pointed questions. First, why do the Nazis hate you so much? And second, why should we take your side against our allies? The Talmudic scholar Rabbi Shatkes was unable to answer the questions in the moment because complete answers involved complex issues of history, economics and religion. However, Rabbi Kalash, the student of human behavior, immediately delivered a single unity-based answer to both questions that stunned the tribunal members because he said, we are Asian like you. His response was so impactful because it transformed the Japanese officers' sense of weaness from one associated with a temporary military alliance to a much more durable bond based on a deeper genetic, cultural and geographic commonality. After a short recess in which tribunal members conferred on the topic, the leader told the rabbis to return to their people with assurances that they had nothing to fear while under the protection of the Japanese government. And so it was.
